chapterTitle: Chapter 0: How To Read This Book
title: How to use the system book
summary: A quick orientation to reading the Tutor architecture book as a practical map instead of a code dump.
transcript: Start here like you would open a map before a long drive. This book is not trying to make you memorize every file. It shows what the learner sees first, which tools power that experience, where local memory lives, and where maintainers should be careful. Read it in order when you are new, or jump by chapter when you are debugging. The point is simple: understand the app as a working learning system, then use Graphify for code-level navigation.

title: The product shape
summary: A lively overview of Tutor as a document study workspace, AI tutor, memory system, and review surface.
transcript: The Product Map chapter is the wide shot. Tutor is not just a PDF viewer, and it is not just a chat box. It is a study workspace where reading, asking, saving, reviewing, and inspecting system behavior belong together. The learner works in Study, the tutor streams help in Chat, the Library keeps reusable learning material, and Admin explains what happened behind the scenes. That product shape is what the rest of the architecture protects.

title: What the tools actually do
summary: A friendly translation of React, Vite, Dexie, Express, models, voice, search, PDF, and Graphify.
transcript: This chapter turns the tool list into plain language. React draws the app. Vite runs it quickly. Zustand remembers live UI state. Dexie stores the learner notebook in the browser. Express handles API calls. Model providers answer, search providers fetch current web facts, and Graphify helps maintainers navigate the code architecture. The useful mental model is not a pile of libraries. It is a relay team, with each tool owning one clear part of the learning experience.

title: How the screen changes
summary: A short guide to App, navigation, lazy views, Zustand state, and context moving across screens.
transcript: Frontend Flow explains how the app moves from one surface to another. App reads the active view from Zustand, then renders Study, Analytics, Revision, or Admin. Heavier views load lazily so the first workspace stays light. The store is the quiet handoff layer: selected text, active document, API keys, usage, voice settings, and active book context can move between screens without turning the UI into a maze. That is why a PDF quote can become a chat question and then a saved revision note.

title: From document to study context
summary: A guide to uploads, PDF/image classification, extraction, and local document grounding.
transcript: Study and ingestion is where raw material becomes usable learning context. The learner uploads a PDF or image, the app classifies what kind of document it is, and extraction turns pages into text and images the tutor can reference. The important boundary is grounding. If the learner asks about the current document, the local source should lead. Ingestion is not just file handling. It is the first trust step before the tutor explains, quotes, summarizes, or creates review material.

title: The live tutor loop
summary: A focused explanation of streaming chat, source-first context, tool calls, web search, flashcards, and voice.
transcript: Chat is the live tutor loop. It gathers the learner message, local context, selected text, and settings, then streams an answer from the server. Tools can search the web when current outside facts are needed, generate flashcards, read page images, or create source cards. The chapter's main lesson is control: local source questions should stay grounded locally, tool results should be visible, and generated study objects should be reviewable before they become trusted learner-brain evidence.

chapterTitle: Chapter 6: Memory, Dexie, And The Library
title: The local learner notebook
summary: A guide to Dexie tables, learning books, entries, evidence, artifacts, corrections, and why chat is not the memory.
transcript: Memory, Dexie, and the Library is the learner notebook chapter. Chat can be cleared, but useful learning state should survive as local records. Dexie stores books, entries, concepts, flashcards, evidence, model runs, tool jobs, retrieval events, artifacts, citations, and corrections. The MemoryOrchestrator is the bridge from a live conversation to durable study material. The trust boundary is the key: generated notes can be useful, but evidence and mastery need stronger, inspectable rows.

title: Review becomes reusable
summary: A guide to built-in books, generated learning books, flashcards, and stored chapter audio guides.
transcript: Revision is where learning stops being trapped in a chat transcript. Built-in books explain the system, generated learning books save what the learner studied, and flashcards turn ideas into active recall. The stored chapter audio guides add a listening path without calling live read-aloud every time. The purpose is practical: come back later, open the Library, and understand the topic again through chapters, concepts, cards, and short audio explanations.

title: Seeing the system work
summary: A guide to Analytics as learner progress and Admin as local beta observability.
transcript: Analytics and Admin answer different questions. Analytics asks: how is the learner doing? It reads local progress, mastery, confidence, interactions, and study activity. Admin asks: what did the system just do? It shows model runs, tool jobs, memory and retrieval rows, evidence, artifacts, citations, corrections, tuning, and diagnostics. Together they keep the app honest. One view serves the learner's progress. The other helps the builder inspect the machinery.

title: One learner turn, end to end
summary: A story-style walkthrough of upload, ask, retrieve, answer, save, and inspect.
transcript: This chapter follows one turn from the inside. A learner uploads a document and asks for help. Study provides context. Chat streams the explanation. Tools may retrieve, search, or generate an artifact. Memory saves useful learning material. Admin records what model ran, which tools fired, and which rows changed. The point of the example is to make the background feel concrete. The app should be able to explain not only the lesson, but how the lesson was produced.

title: Which model does what
summary: A map of tutor chat, fallbacks, learning-book updates, vision, web search, voice, stored guides, and pricing.
transcript: The model map keeps provider behavior understandable. Chat usually goes through OpenRouter. Learning-book updates and trace explanations use model calls too. Vision reads pages or images. Web search is reserved for current outside facts. Live voice and read-aloud have their own provider boundaries. Stored chapter guides are different: they are generated ahead of time and checked in as MP3s. The map matters because every model call should have a job, a cost, and a reason to exist.

title: Navigation and safety rails
summary: A guide to Graphify-first code navigation, debug surfaces, and safety boundaries.
transcript: Graphify, debugging, and safety is the maintainer's guardrail chapter. Graphify is the repo architecture graph, not the learner brain. It helps agents route changes before opening a pile of files. Debugging surfaces show what the app recorded. Safety boundaries say what should not happen silently: hidden model internals, ungrounded mastery changes, destructive corrections, or cloud assumptions before beta. The theme is disciplined visibility. Move fast, but leave a trail the team can inspect.

title: Where to be careful
summary: A short guide to high-risk files, local verification, browser QA, and preserving product boundaries.
transcript: Maintenance Boundaries tells you where to slow down. Content books are easier to edit. Dexie schema, server contracts, Zustand state, chat streaming, document ingestion, and generated Graphify artifacts are more sensitive. The chapter's job is not to scare you. It is to make verification proportional to risk. Small copy change? Keep it focused. Shared state or API behavior? Run broader checks. Visual change? Open the app. Good maintenance is just attention with evidence.

title: Quick tour of the learner brain
summary: A lively guide to the foreground tutor, local learner-brain ledger, and background worker contract.
transcript: Here is the big picture. LearningAI is trying to feel like one attentive tutor, while still keeping its promises in a very inspectable way. On the surface, the learner gets explanations, questions, document help, and review material. Underneath that, the local learner brain keeps the evidence, artifacts, corrections, retrieval traces, and mastery movement. The key is the contract between those layers. The live tutor can adapt quickly, but durable memory has to earn its way into the ledger.

title: The ledger is the trust layer
summary: A guide to durable learner state, typed records, sources, and correction paths.
transcript: This chapter is the trust layer. A good tutor can be warm and flexible in the moment, but permanent learner state needs stricter rules. Every durable row should answer four questions: what changed, why it changed, what supports it, and how someone can inspect or correct it. That is why generated notes, cards, source cards, and audio guides all sit in explicit artifact and citation records. They can be useful before they are fully verified. The app should never confuse a helpful generated object with proof.

title: Fast teaching, careful memory
summary: A walkthrough of live tutoring adaptation versus evidence-gated memory updates.
transcript: The teaching loop is where the app should feel most human. It can explain, notice a learner struggling, switch examples, ask a quick check, and schedule recall. Those signals should shape the next tutor move immediately, because that is what makes a lesson feel responsive. Durable memory is different. If the system wants to change mastery or long-term learner state, it needs an auditable evidence row. The lesson can move quickly, but the learner-brain ledger moves carefully.

title: Grounding is the main boundary
summary: An energetic guide to local-source-first retrieval, generated artifacts, and visible citation states.
transcript: Retrieval and citations are the honesty boundary. If the learner is asking about a page, a selection, an uploaded document, or a saved learning book, local source material should come first. Web search is for current outside facts or explicit requests, not for replacing the learner's own material. Generated artifacts and citations have visible states, from ready and not checked to verified or conflicting. The point is to show exactly how much trust has actually been earned.

title: Admin is the operating room
summary: A clear guide to model, tool, memory, retrieval, evidence, artifact, correction, and tuning surfaces.
transcript: Admin is the operating room for local beta. It should answer practical questions: what happened during this tutor request, which model ran or failed, which tools were called, which retrieval and memory rows changed, and which artifacts need review. Runtime tuning is the companion to that visibility. The user gets local controls for source versus web behavior, memory context, tool budget, and refresh cadence. Admin reports recorded state. It should be useful and humble about what the system actually knows.

title: Voice should respect timing
summary: A natural explanation of stored chapter guides, live voice boundaries, and playback controls.
transcript: Voice is powerful only when it respects timing. Live tutoring speech needs quick starts, quick stops, short turns, and honest fallback to text. Library chapters are different. They should not call a live read-aloud route every time someone presses play. The better pattern is a short authored guide, generated once, stored as a local asset, and played with normal controls for pause and speed. The audio is there to explain the chapter, not to recite every paragraph.

title: Prove it locally before cloud
summary: A roadmap guide for proving learner-brain behavior before AWS or production sync work.
transcript: The roadmap is deliberately grounded. First, prove the learner-brain behavior in the browser: evidence gates, ledgers, model runs, tool jobs, correction overlays, artifact provenance, diagnostics export, and stored audio. Once those pieces behave well locally, then cloud sync, tenant isolation, backups, and production dashboards can enter the story. The local app should earn trust before infrastructure grows around it, because cloud scale only helps if the underlying behavior is worth scaling.

title: Shared words keep the system honest
summary: A closing guide to precise vocabulary, trusted references, and source boundaries.
transcript: The glossary chapter is not decorative. Shared words keep a complicated learning system understandable. Evidence means an observed learner action or source-linked event. A mastery delta is a recorded movement, not a mood. A correction is a visible review intent, not a silent rewrite. A learner brain is a local ledger, not a mysterious cloud memory. When the team uses precise terms, the product can stay ambitious without getting slippery.

title: How the surfaces connect
summary: A guide to the reader's wireframe map and the relationship between Study, Chat, Library, Admin, and background systems.
transcript: Wireframe Connections is the design map. It shows how the learner moves through Study, Chat, Library, Admin, and the background systems that support them. The point is not decoration. It is orientation. A good interface should make the main routes feel obvious, while still letting advanced surfaces like Admin and Graphify exist without confusing them with the learner brain. This chapter helps keep the product shape visible.

title: Two moods, one product
summary: A guide to Cosmic Obsidian for active study and paper-style calm for Revision and documentation.
transcript: Theme System explains the app's two-mood design. Study, Chat, and core AI surfaces use Cosmic Obsidian: dark surfaces, neon accents, glass, and motion. Revision and documentation become quieter, with paper color, serif type, and notebook texture. That contrast is intentional. Active learning can feel futuristic and alive, while review should feel calm enough to read. The design system protects both energies without turning the app into a visual argument.

title: Small components, real behavior
summary: A guide to rendered previews that make buttons, cards, panels, and controls easier to reason about.
transcript: UI Component Snapshots turn design rules into visible examples. Instead of describing every component in abstract language, this chapter shows compact rendered pieces: cards, controls, panels, charts, and interaction states. The value is practical. When the app grows, snapshots help keep style decisions consistent. They also make regressions easier to see, because a component that looked calm, readable, and aligned yesterday should still feel that way after today's code change.

title: Controls that explain the system
summary: A guide to local beta controls for tuning, diagnostics, source checks, correction overlays, and stored audio guides.
transcript: Local Beta Control Patterns is about trust through controls. Runtime settings, diagnostics exports, source checks, correction overlays, artifact provenance, and stored audio guides all need clear UI. The user should see what happened, what can be tuned, and what is still only local beta. This chapter is the product design version of the learner-brain rule: do useful things, but make the status visible. Control without clarity is just another hidden system.
